transcript.transcript: All right, we are recording. And I'm gonna start this. And you interrupt me somewhere if there's something else you want to say that sort of come to mind, all right? Okay. So we started talking about your family history and I know you and I discussed this when we had the initial interview. You're originally from what part of the country? Alabama. Okay. And how many siblings and what's your birth order? Well, I'm the youngest and I have three siblings, all deceased now, who were seven, nine, and 12 years older than me. Change of life baby? I'm sorry? Change of life baby? Well, my mother had had two miscarriages and a stillbirth between my sister Josephine and myself. So I was often referred to as the golden child. I can imagine. Because I was everybody's baby. And I had three other parents. Well, I shouldn't say three. I had two other parents, my two sisters. And what ages were they, older than you? Seven and nine. Oh, so your brother was the oldest? My brother was the oldest, and he went into the military right out of high school. Well he was a senior in high school and I was in first grade and he had to walk me to school and he made me walk on the other side of the street. [Laughter] Oh, that's funny. Which he denied. Now, as you all got older, did you become, were you close when you were young? I guess you were close with your sisters. Did you become closer? Was that age difference always an issue? Oh no, we were very close. We were all very close. And I think my brother was, my brother, well he said that I would say what he was thinking. Because he was a very, not really blunt and that kind of thing. But he and I were close. I don't think your blunt. I think you're open. I can be blunt. So can I but I like that. I mean, at least everyone knows how you stand. That's what I always say. It's kind of... my mother told me once, just before she spanked me, for lying to her about something. And she said to me, 'you're not a good liar. Don't do it. Everybody knows you're lying. You're scratching your head. You're looking around.' [Laughter] I was like maybe seven years old or something and it kind of stuck to me because I'm not a good liar. [Laughs] Well it's too hard. You have to remember too many things. Yes, absolutely. That's funny. And what did your parents do? Was your mother a stay-at-home mom? My mother was a stay-at-home mom. My dad worked for a factory in Alabama that produced bricks and this kind of thing. I know he drove a, what do you call those? Forklift? Forklift and all of that. But he was actually a farmer. Oh, really? And they moved to a town near, a city near Birmingham: Bessemer, Alabama. And this company that he worked for was owned by Grace Kelly's family. HarbisonWalker Refractories. One more time? HarbisonWalker Refractories, I think was the name of it. And what were your parents' names? Rachel. Rachel Ann and Otto Samuel. And both also African-American? Mmm-hmm How did they meet? They were introduced by a mutual friend at church. And my dad was eight years older than my mother. Good marriage? Oh annoyingly good. [Laughter] Okay. They never fought? Well not in front of us, not in front of me, but as my siblings said 'that I had different parents.' Well, I think that's true. Yeah. I'm a middle child with a sister seven years older and seven years younger. Okay. And my younger sister and I definitely had a different set of parents than my older sister. For one thing, she was an only child. Well, that's how I felt too. Yeah, because-- I think you were. I was. My last sister left home to go to school, and my mother always said that I had a nervous breakdown after she left because she took me everywhere with her. She was a good sister. Well I asked her were you taking me with you so you didn't get questioned about where? She would just be at her friend's house. They'd be playing records. They'd give me a comic book to read. I'm over there. Because I said I had two musics. I knew all of her music from the 50s and all of my music from the 60s and I used to make, when it was the thing, I'd make these little tapes for her. And I can remember who her favorites were and that kind of thing and I actually got her tickets to see Elvis Presley. I didn't know she was gonna be out of the country when he was at the Civic Center then. So I ended up, my husband and I ended up going. Was it a great show? Yeah, yeah. He was always a showman. It was Fat Elvis, but-- Yeah, I know, well. And he wasn't doing all that moving like-- And that sister's name was? Josephine Johnson The next sister? Mary. And your brother? Fancher. F-A-N-C-H-E-R. Is that a family name? Mm-hmm. And you moved up north when? 1964. And why? College. Oh, you came up without your family? No, my sister, Josephine, was already here. Oh, okay. Was she going to school in this area? She went to Hartford Hospital and University. Well, it wasn't the University of Hartford that when she went, but-- Women's college or something? Yeah. Hilliard. Yeah, Hilliard, that's the name. So she was here and established. Was she married? Yeah. So you lived with them? Yes. I had visited her in like 1959 or 1960, I spent the summer with her, babysitting her oldest. I was like 12. Why did your sister come here? We lived in the Jim Crow South. Everybody left. A lot went back from what I see too. Yeah, That's what we're going to talk about that a little bit. Yeah, that to me is fascinating. I mean that was, I wanted to get out of it. Yeah. I was lucky because, well, that's another whole story. Because my, I don't know if you've heard of the separate but equal, that was their excuse for. Oh, yeah. Yeah. I'm going to, just so you know, I'm an American historian, but I was studying to be an American Studies professor. Oh, okay. So yeah, I know. Yeah. So, I was fortunate enough to go to a regional high school that was a model high school for. Was it integrated? No. Oh, I was gonna say, my God. No, so that they could point to this school. Oh, it was, see we're giving everybody a good education. We had everything. I mean, we had, I graduated in 1964. We had a language lab. Okay. We even had a symphony orchestra. Name it, we had it, okay? And was it just luck that you ended up going there, or because of where you lived? It was because of where I lived. Kids from three towns. And it wasn't even the city that I lived in, it was the location in the city, because there was another school that was like in walking distance from our house, but for whatever reason our address fell into this. So I had to take a bus to school in another town but I was very fortunate. I mean we had wonderful science and math curriculums at that time. And teachers: black/white? The schools were segregated. So the teaching as well. Yes. Which I look back on now as good at that time for where I lived, because the competition, you could be who you were. You weren't in that situation of having to think about, I mean, looking back at it now. Well, let me ask you about what you think about our schools now in town, because we were named the All-America City because we were an integrated community, And we're at a point now where I don't know if we were at one point the most segregated high school in the state. I believe it. I mean, as I said, I've seen three generations of kids. My niece's, Meredith, Candice, my nephew Victor, graduated from Bloomfield schools in the 70s, early 80s, and it was a different school. My kids, it was, I could look at the pictures and see it's becoming, because you did have a lot of white flight here in terms of the schools. You had white flight and you had bright flight after awhile. Well we were thinking about my son going to, what's the Ritzy school in Avon? Because he is a real smart kid. I would say, we used to say, if he'd show up in class, he'd get a B. If he did anything, get an A, that kind of, he and his little friend. Every year when they did the testing, they brought the whole school average up. But he was nuts. [Laughs] We didn't know it then, but ADHD kind of thing. He could do, as his second grade teacher at our first conference with her, she said, 'you know, your son is brilliant. And he would be the one who discovered a cure for cancer, but nobody would ever know it because he couldn't find his notes.' Oh gee. [Laughter] And that was a perfect description. Yeah. So back to the school and as the school system changed. So now we are a predominantly non-white school system. Right. Do you think that that's a detriment to the kids going to it? I do. Because? We live in a diverse world. And I don't think this shows that. I like for my grandkids to be around a lot of different kinds of people. And that's, but you know what's interesting-- And that's what makes growth. So what Bloomfield celebrates is its diversity. So that, yeah, that's-- I know. Well it is. I mean it is. In one demographic it's diverse. It's diverse in residency. Yeah. Depending on where you live. Yeah. Well, it's still, I mean, you've been here long enough to see that the diversity that you see in the east end of town has spread to the west end of town. Not quite as much as, but that's just how things go. You go from an urban environment, I think you and I talked about this in the first conversation, to a semi-suburban and then as you make more money or the family gets bigger you move to a nicer neighborhood and a bigger house and then very often you move out of town and a lot of people move out of town because of the school system, because of the high school, but when I was going here, it was the same thing. It wasn't necessarily because of a color thing. People just felt that the school system wasn't that great. They wanted to go to Northwest Catholic. That was the big thing. Everyone after middle school. My theory always was that there were just enough non-white kids in the school who were afraid we were going to date each other. And that's why they left. And I don't know if I was right or wrong, but that was certainly the feeling I had. I think you're right. I agree. And I can remember, I mean, just looking at the pictures, you know, you can see as it changes over and looking at the group picture now with my five-year-old grandson, They had a little group picture. I think there was one Asian and one white kid in the whole group, and all of the other kids, I don't know if they were black or Hispanic or what, but... Don't have a really big Hispanic population, which is one reason why we're considered to be more segregated, because very often Hispanics are considered to be white. Well, they might self-describe themselves, depending on, since that's what the census calls for now. Yeah. [Laughter] This is so good. I hope you're enjoying this, because I'm really enjoying this. It's interesting. Well, it's a learning experience for me because I was supposed to be sitting in your chair. I know. And I'm going to be interviewing a couple of people and I have to go through the training now that I'm healthier. It's, I'll tell you, it's something that the first interview I did, this is a much better interview that we're having right now, and it's just sort of learning. I have to shut up sometimes, so if I'm talking too much, tell me, okay? That's okay. Okay, let's see. Did your parents have a vision of who they thought you should be and what they wanted you to do? Or did that influence what you became? Sure. Absolutely. Well, my dad, as chauvinistic as he was in his own way, was very precise about his daughters receiving college education. Because? So that they could take care of themselves. And my mother was also very much encouraging that too. My parents were not educated people, but they were self-educated. Did they get through high school? No. My mother had a very traumatic experience when she was 13 years old, not quite 13 years old. She was walking home from school. She lived in Selma, Alabama. She was born there. And she was walking home from school, and to sum it up, she was attacked by a carload of white boys and left on the road for dead. Her father and her brothers went looking for her when she didn't get home and they found her and they brought her to a local, the nearest hospital. She had a fractured skull. And well, my grandfather, her father had a reputation for being crazy, which is how some black people survived. And he had a wife, he was born in slavery. He ran away, took up with the Union Army, was what they called a buffalo soldier, returned. From all I can figure out, I think he had like two or three wives. Different places? No, at the same place. And they were all, and he hated white people because his father was white. A rape? Who knows? Well, this is, you said-- He was born during slavery. Well, could it have been a love match? I doubt it, but. Yeah, I do too. But, took her to this hospital, and they told him to take her to the colored hospital, and he refused, which probably saved her life. She was in a coma for several weeks. They ended up doing whatever surgery, and I always remember there was a spot in her about that big right up here where hair wouldn't grow and she said, I would like to brush her hair because she had beautiful hair, and I can remember she would say 'that's where they put the plate in my head.' And I just had this vision as a little kid of a dinner plate. [Laughter] So, let me see, and make sure I've got this right. So, it's your grandfather or her grandfather that was born into slavery? Her father. My grandfather. Okay. Did you know your grandfather at all? No, all of my grandparents were deceased when I was born. Okay. I never met any of them. But there are lots of stories about... My mother's mother was probably about 40 years younger than him. Okay? Okay. But he didn't look, the pictures I've seen of him, he didn't look his age. And he obviously didn't act his age. [Laughter] Well, it's kind of funny because I was really into the ancestry thing and pulling up the census reports. Two of the census describe him as mulatto. And the last one says negro. Well, and that, because of the way our terminology changes, right? Right. Yeah. And I thought that- Was he light-skinned? Yeah. And there was, well on my mother's side, supposedly, there was Indian-Native American blood, and she did have that look, kind of like the, and all of that. High cheek bone? There's always a question about, because every black person says they have Native American. And right now at the casinos that's a good thing to have. Not the right tribe. Evidently for us. [Laughter] But because this was the first time that I had heard of what was referred to as the Trail of Tears. It was when the Native Americans were marched from Florida to the Southeast. Right, Andrew Jackson. The story was that a lot of them were hidden by the enslaved people, and they passed for-- Black, which is better than being an Indian at that point. Right. What was high school like for you? You said it was a great school. You said it was a great school. What was high school like for me? The nerd. You were the nerd? Okay. Well, I got along with everybody, but I wasn't part of any there were the pretty girls and the-- Did you have close friends when you were in high school? Oh yeah, absolutely. But I wasn't attached. I could float among all the groups. Do you go back to any reunions? How have things changed as far as the groups go? Well, there was a boy that I liked very much that I thought would probably end up in prison. And I got a message, I hadn't seen these people in 20 years, right? And really kind of lost contact with everybody except maybe one or two people. And this young man, his friend that was there told me, 'oh well Bobby really wanted to be here but he was already scheduled to go to some physician's conference in Switzerland.' I said 'he's a doctor?' [Laughter] I knew he was smart, but he was just one of those trouble... Really? [Laughter] Have you gone back to any more recent ones? The last one I went to was, I think 2004. It was right after my husband passed away. We had already signed up to go, because it was a cruise that was leaving from New Orleans to Mexico. Oh, what a great idea for a reunion. And I wasn't going to go after that, but I hated to lose the money because we'd already put a deposit and my girlfriend made me go and she went with me Was it a good experience? It was a wonderful experience. It was the best time. Do you mind my asking about your husband's passing? No, I don't mind. Was he ill long? No. He had a stroke. And, I mean, he did have, he was 58, he had some blood pressure issues which we thought, he was on medication and stuff and so he had a stroke in the middle of the night and that's when I learned about a stroke. I thought it happened and then it was, it's like you had it and I didn't know it was continued, the continuation of how it impacts you. Because he was fine. He was on the floor. I said, 'you fell out of bed?' And he got up and he was like that, but he was able to talk and by the time the ambulance got there he was not able to speak one side was paralyzed. He lived for about a week. It had to be very rough. Huh? It had to be very rough, I would think. Yeah. That's a young man, really. Well, he was a robust kind of guy and never really sick or anything. Yeah, well blood pressure, they talk about a silent killer, that's a silent killer. Yeah, absolutely, and that was a difficult time for me. I can imagine. Very difficult. I had a 14 year old. So your kids are still at home, right? And as my husband said, 'I would be pissed off with him until the day he died because of the age.' Like we couldn't have done this in our twenties? But I think in some ways when you're older you have more resources. Oh yeah. And financially. I think that we're probably, me personally, I think I was a better parent because what he would always say to me, 'what's gonna happen when you find something that you can't fix? Because you think you can fix everybody.' We're very much alike. Yep. Of course, just listen and I'll just keep on telling you how to do it. Yeah, do what I told you. And well, he was the first thing I couldn't fix. Yeah. Because I just, I don't know, I just had it in my head that he would be okay. And well, it's a shock at that age, something sudden like that. Yeah, well, that wasn't the first time that it happened in our family. My father had a heart attack at age 58. Really? Wow. And he passed? He died instantly. See, for the person that happens to, that's wonderful. It's for the rest of us, it's... Yeah, I said, well, don't have to linger. And my brother had a heart attack at 58. I'm not comfortable with that age. Well, you're well beyond it. Yeah, but at the time. Yeah, I know. And that was a hard time. Do you think that they didn't get the medical care that they should have? Do you think that was part of it? Or just we didn't know enough back then? I don't think we knew enough. I don't think we knew. I definitely knew my dad. I mean, my father was diagnosed with high blood pressure in his 40s, late 40s, and my mother was the health food fanatic. All that soul food that they talk about. Never had it? We had it on special occasions, but everything before that was broiled or grilled or a lot of vegetables. And we're drinking water before it was popular. Eight glasses of water every day. That kind of thing. My husband was diagnosed when he was 40 with essential hypertension. We think he had it in his 20s. We just never, he never went to a doctor before that, and that could be, because sometimes you can't do anything unless there's medication. But I don't know. I think that part of the problem is educating ourselves too. Do you cook like your mom cooked? Sort of? Well, the biggest disappointment of my husband's life was that I couldn't cook. Oh really? Did he cook? Yeah. So do you cook now? Well, I have to but it was because he was from here. He was born and raised here. And really didn't know anything about Southern, that kind of lifestyle because his parents were from here and-- How many generations was he up here, in the Hartford area, do you know? Well his mother's parents came from I'm trying to think of somewhere down near New London, I've forgotten where. And now his father's parents came from Alabama, but his father was born here. And I really don't know the rest of their history beyond that. A couple of, well I know his, I met his grandmother on his father's side, but all of his mother's people from here. And you met where? You and your husband? At a bar. Well, there you go. [Laughter] No, actually, it was kind of a funny thing because we had, there used to be this place on Capen Street, I can't think of the name of it now, but it was just a little joint pizza and stuff like that. And I worked around the corner from there at McCook Hospital at the time UConn had taken over. And some of the girls, the nurses and the other, I was a desk clerk or whatever you call it back then, I worked in the emergency room and we sometimes after work they would go to this place, have a pizza and beer or whatever. And... Well, that's not really a bar. No, well... It's a good story, though. It was. I mean... Okay. And he...they persuaded me to come, because I usually didn't go. And, because I didn't like the owner, who was kind of fresh. Did he like you? He was kind of fresh. Yeah, I hear you. Okay, I gotcha. Okay. [Laughter] How old were you at this point? I think I was about 22. Yeah. And this guy comes in, and he says, someone driving whatever kind of car, what was a Volkswagen, something, the lights were on, back when they didn't turn off automatically. And it was one of the ladies at our table and she said 'oh that's my car' and she said 'let me go shut it--' and he said 'I'll shut it off for you' and she gave him her keys and when he went out the door I said 'are you nuts he's probably halfway to 91 now with your car.' [Laughter] So he comes back in with the keys and she said 'let me buy you a drink' and he said 'no let me buy you ladies a drink.' And he comes and sits down by me and starts talking. I didn't talk to him, because I said, 'really pushy.' And so he knew where we worked and oh my first words to him were very insulting because he had told he had just gotten like maybe a couple weeks out of the VA hospital. He had been injured in Vietnam and he had been in the hospital for six months, not expected to walk again. And I said, 'oh that's why I admire Muhammad Ali. You, a black man, are not even free in your own country and you're over there killing the yellow man for the white man, really?' That was the first thing I said to him. But he must have liked it? And he said, 'number one, I enlisted, I wasn't drafted. I love my country, and anything you wanna know about my politics or religion, why don't you give me your number, and I'll call you.' That is a great line. And he was sitting there, and I just turned around like that, and until we left, I never said another word to him and he showed up at my job the next day because he knew where we were. He was smitten obviously. Yeah. So he stalked me for about [Laughter]. Because he knew all the doctors and some of the other staff too because they were also at the VA Hospital. And so I got these people coming up to me and 'Barbara he's a nice guy won't you--.' So finally I agreed to go have coffee with him. And the rest is history? The rest, well, he said to me, he took me to this diner in New Britain that was across the street from New Britain Hospital. Yeah? General Hospital at the time and he said that he used to come there after school before he went to his job at New Britain General Hospital and have apple pie a la mode which he ordered and I think he must have gone there and set it up and he said and I play this song and think about you. And he played "My Girl" on the little-- Oh, gee. Oh, wow. As I sat there like-- Did you marry him two weeks later? About two years later. Oh, gee. Wow. What else did you need, Barbara? I was difficult. That's pretty romantic. I was difficult. So I'm curious, politically, did you end up being more in sync than you thought, or was he a lot more? No, he was very conservative, and I was out there. Yeah. Okay. I had to train him. I think, well, actually, he got to be more, well, I should say less conservative, and I got to be more conservative. I think that's what happens when you're with somebody and there's a difference. So what year were you married? What year did you marry? We married in 1972, I think. What year is this? 2023. 1973. Yeah. That's a lovely story actually. [Laughs] It really is. Yeah, he got me with that, that he said, I used to think about you, not a girl like you, or somebody like you. He was a BS artist, let me tell you. [Laughter] I'm married to one of those, I know what that's like. Never ending entertainment. Was he a flirt? No. Just a charmer. He never met any strangers. I'll put it that way. Were you religious, either of you? Would you describe yourself that way? Yeah. Does religion play a large part in your life, do you think? Well-- And has influenced you? Yeah. I would say growing up in the black church, because growing up in the South, that was the outlet for a lot of things that--the first dance that I went to was a church sponsored dance. Well, I'm African Methodist. AME, right? Uh-huh. And our theory is that Jesus turned that water into wine for a reason. [Laughs] Everything in moderation. So just a lot of activities: learning to stand up in front of people and talk and debate teams and that kind of thing. Now my belief is I really don't, your faith, that's personal. To me the church activity is more social than religious, if that makes...I don't need to go to church to know right from wrong. And your husband, was he religious at all? My husband grew up in the Pentecostal church. And his great uncle started a church in Hartford. Which one? Well, I can't think of what the name of it was then, but it was Latter Rain, something now. His cousin took it over, but he was the heir apparent as the oldest male in the family. He always said to me, he left the church because 99 and a half wouldn't do, you had to be 100% perfect. He knew he was never going to hit that mark. But he was very ecumenical. He would go to church with me. He might be at the mosque. He might be down at the Jewish, because he was a member of the credit union. Anywhere, wherever he felt like going on any-- He sounds like a great guy. I can't think of anybody who didn't like him. He just was that kind of person. He was hard not to like. And yet he went to war which is really interesting. Well I know for a fact that he joined the army to get away from his mother. Well, that makes a lot more sense, okay. So that he could--well he told me that-- Too demanding? Too deamnding. She saw him as the head of the family. Yeah. That's why I said he didn't really have an opportunity to be a kid. He had so many responsibilities. Yeah, that does actually make a lot of sense. Yeah, and being a responsible kind of person that he was. Well, and he said that he was told that he wasn't college material. And so he didn't see an outlet for himself, working at whatever, I can't remember where. Factory. He didn't see that as the life that he wanted. He was in the military for like seven years. He was discharged because of the injury. He had planned to make a career of it, and that's what a lot of black males did. It was an opportunity that you didn't have in general society. Right. Okay, now we're going to go into the Bloomfield specific questions. Okay. I'm enjoying talking to you, I really am. I'm glad you're going to have a chance to do the interviews. They are really, they really give you an insight into the other person that you're talking to. Anyway, now you said you moved to Bloomfield because of the housing, is that right? We moved to Bloomfield, I was working for the city of Hartford. We had bought a house in Hartford. Because at that time, there was a residency requirement. I just figured I'd be working there until I retired. [Laughs] As I think a lot of people had that attitude. My house was broken into. We had gone to, I think we had gone to New York to see a play on a bus trip or something, and we got back, and the house had been broken into. As the police told us, we were lucky because some other houses had been broken into and they damaged the house. But I never- Where were you living then, Barbara? What street were you on? Branford Street, right off of Coventry Street. I mean, had you thought that was a tough neighborhood or? No, it was a very safe, we were the youngest people on the street. Everybody else was, we were the kids. What year was that? Did this happen? I think, around 1974. I'm trying to remember when did we move to. It doesn't matter. I'm just trying to get a chronology. Yeah. And I never, after that happened, I was in the basement doing laundry in my pajamas and the little window, and this was like 11 o'clock at night. I looked up and there was a face looking back at me. Yeah, that'll give you a heart attack. And after that, I was not ever comfortable in the house. Do you think the neighborhood was changing somehow? It was just what was going on in general? I don't know. I mean, the neighborhood seemed stable enough. It wasn't like people were moving in and out and as I said we when we moved there we were the youngest people there and there the least amount of time. Our neighbor had lived there for like 30 years, the other, and all around. But it just, once you get that feeling that you're not safe and whatever, and that's when we moved to Bloomfield. We were looking at houses and we were being steered to certain areas because we were looking. Before we bought the house we lived over on New Britain Avenue. And I loved that area, I loved over there. I wanted, we were looking at houses on Fairfield Avenue and stuff, and we were actually steered to the Blue Hills. How did you know they were steering you? I mean, they just, I'm asking because I want to, they said, 'oh, this isn't available, this is the house you'd really like.' I mean, were they subtle? Yeah. Well, I guess they were being, thought they were being subtle. But it wasn't at all. Yeah. And we changed realtors three times. Were the realtors white? We had two white realtors and one black one. And was that the difference when they were not really? Yeah, that's interesting. I think that, well, the black realtor told us that there were some really nice houses that we could afford, in a certain area. And he also said, there's no point in you looking at those houses on Fairfield Avenue. And I think he was right. That they wouldn't have sold to you? Right. At that time. So what year did you buy, Latimer, is where you moved? No. I've had three houses in Bloomifled. [Laughs] So the first house you bought when? About 1975 I think it was. Yeah, Carpenter Lane around the corner from where I live now. And then the next house? And the next house was Duncaster Road. I remember this on the, when we talked-- We lived out there for 20 years. And when did you buy that house? Do you remember? I'm thinking about 1984, something like that. And then the last move was after your husband passed? Right, where I am now. And that was when? Latimer Lane. That was in 2006. I wasn't comfortable out there in those woods by myself. [Laughs] I can really, it's a lovely area out there, but it raises-- And our house had too many ways you could get in there. [Laughs] On too many levels. [Laughs] Has your view of Bloomfield changed since you've been here? Do you see it as the same kind of community that brought you here? We came here because we wanted the diversity that existed here, or that was supposed to exist here. And I think it was a good decision. Because the one thing that, especially that we wanted for our kids was for them to see all kinds of people and not feel that they were cornered. That somebody was gonna look at them funny if they went into a store or whatever. Yeah. And did that happen? Did they feel that they were accepted? Oh, they loved Bloomfield. They loved Bloomfield. But no one came back here, they've all moved on somewhere else, right? Well, my daughter is living with me and she would like to find something here. Tough market right now. But, yeah, well she's making, in her 30s, a life change, going to a nursing school. Oh, that's fantastic. What was she doing before that? Hair. Well, they're both personal care in different ways. Mm-hmm. But I mean, she went to a hair academy or whatever, and she was always very talented with hair. But she loves it here. And does she have children also or no? She has three children. They're also with you? Uh-huh. God bless you. [Laughter] Okay. Well, she has an 11-year-old, a 10-year-old, and a 5-year-old. And boys, girls? Two boys and one girl. Which one's the girl? The middle one. The middle bossy one. Hey. If you don't stand up for yourself, Barbara, who's going to stand up for you? Absolutely. Okay. Oh, this little girl is, she should have her own TV program. She's so dramatic. [Laughter] Well, the age too, that's, yeah. Yeah. And she's exactly like her mother. Well, you did a good job then, right? Exactly like her. I mean, she looks like her. My daughter was very talented. She actually went on American Idol. Oh your kidding? Wow. And she made it to, I didn't know they had about seven layers of judges to go through before they made it to the ones on TV. They showed a little clip of her before they kicked her off. She got that far? She got that far and she didn't really start out to go she went to wherever they were having the auditions with a friend of hers and who like how these girls hang together. Yeah, and they persuaded her to go and that was how she decided to do it. She's saying to me, it was so many people there. She said: 'ma you know I don't like crowds and people telling me to do this and do that.' But it was a great experience for her. I'm gonna ask you a little bit about what activities or events do you think brought people from different backgrounds together in town? Or brought you in contact with people from different backgrounds? When you moved into town, were you living in an area that was fairly integrated? Oh yeah. I think that shopping used to bring people here from different backgrounds. Oh, what was that Sunset Sounds? Cigna, yeah. Cigna. Do you think the school, because you had kids in the school, were you friendly with the other parents necessarily? Some. But as a, I have to say, my husband was more active with the kids during that time than I was, because he had a schedule that allowed him to be able to do things. And he, I mean, we both participated in stuff, but he just really loved it. Well, those are the people that should be raising the children. The ones that really love it. I mean, I loved it too. Yeah, I know. But I had a very demanding job at the time. And what was kind of, I can remember people saying, 'oh,' after he passed, 'oh, I just remember your husband every time I saw him, had those kids with him.' He would go to the programs at the library. He was a good dad, wasn't he? Oh yeah, he was an excellent dad. He loved it and that's why he said I'd be pissed off with him until the day he died, because he didn't know how much he would like it. [Laughs] He was a scout leader. He got into it. He was growing canaries and birds in the basement. Oh my god, really? Wow! The kids were selling them to, the scouts, were selling them to pet shops. Do you have any memories of certain businesses or landmarks in town that were really important to the family, or to you? You talked about Sunset Sounds, I know. Oh yeah, COPACO. Did you take them to the petting zoo? Oh yes, I think I got pictures of them there, absolutely. And I can remember, actually, I think, yeah, I'm getting old, because I can't remember some things, but I can remember the cows. It seems like we bought like a half a-- Cow? You probably did if you had a freezer. Yeah. They used to sell it that way. Because my mom wanted to, my mom lived with me for a while, and she was into that, cooking. But I tell you, I didn't know how to cook when I got married, because I could bake. I was a good baker. But you were the baby. Usually the baby doesn't have the housekeeping skills the other kids have for some reason. I can clean a house. My skill, laundry, things like that, but I was not cooking. Well you weren't interested? I don't know if I was interested or not. My mother would let me help her prep, but she just really was, probably didn't want me messing up the kitchen. She was the queen of the kitchen. But, so his biggest thing, my husband, was that he married this southern girl that didn't know how to cook soul food. [Laughs] But you had an explanation for it? And didn't eat it. No, I did not eat it, very little, because I was used to the style that my mother was cooking for my- - How did she learn to cook that way? Did her mother cook that way, or is it something she read that it was nutritious? She was, I can't, my mother was into this healthy lifestyle, and there was a couple of books, I can't remember the names now, You Are What You Eat was one of them. I can't think of-- So this was something that she just sort of came to do? She was doing this because my father with the blood pressure. Ah, okay. So we were fed a lot of baked and not frying stuff or I mean that was a big treat. Yeah, when you talk about self-educated, you're not kidding. That's exactly what she was to know that. Was she into like natural remedies and things like that too, or just the cooking? No, because she had a relative that was into natural stuff, and she thought it was crap. [Laughs] My mother's mother was a midwife. Oh, okay. So, and I can remember, because my oldest sister was a preemie And she weighed under three pounds. And she lived? Oh yeah. Wow. She lived to the age of 75 and she had cancer. But I can remember my mother saying, had it not been for her mother, she probably wouldn't have survived because she said that her mother taught her to feed her with a medicine dropper. Because she would not nurse. Because my mother used to tell her, you've been trouble all your life. [Laughter] My older sister told me I was adopted. They told me they found me on the doorstep. Oh yeah, me too. Well, actually, that or the postman, maybe. Siblings are lovely, aren't they? Love them. Me too, but. Well, the main lesson that I learned from my husband about kids, stay out of their mess unless you see blood. There you go, and he was a wise man. Absolutely right. Let them work it out. Okay, got a couple more questions. We'll wrap up. Is there anything in particular you would like people don't know about to know about the town? I think that Bloomfield has all of the same issues and problems that exist anywhere. There's racism in Bloomfield. There's kindness in Bloomfield. There's helpfulness in Bloomfield. Does anything set Bloomfield apart in the way they respond to it? Does anything set Bloomfield apart in the way that they respond to it? I think that Bloomfield has a lot of issues that needs to come to the forefront. Like what Barbara? Education. This hasn't been, I think, a great school system since maybe the 1970s. And why do you think that is? I don't know. I don't have an answer for it. We certainly spend money. Yeah, but when you throw money at a problem, it doesn't necessarily fix the problem. There's something, I don't know. I don't have an answer. Do you think it's the entire school system or just the higher level? Pockets. Yeah. Pockets, I think. Because it used to, they used to say, 'well, it's a great system when the kids are young. But once they get to middle school, it's just.' I don't know. I don't have an answer. And maybe that's kind of a lousy. Well, I'm not expecting you to have one. No ,you can know an issue, but not necessarily-- I could see it from when I worked for the town for 25 years. I wasn't working for the Board of Education, but I did have a lot of interaction with them in my job. And I don't know what the answers are. I think there was a time when people were interested in finding the answer. Now it's just status quo. Do you think that that's just the political environment in general, or do you think it's us in particular? I think part of it is political environment. Politicians do a lot of talking, but very little acting, in my opinion. And I think that's, I'm not saying that about all of them. No, I know that. But I think that's what politicians do, universally. [Laughs] Of course, politics are not what politics used to be either. Well, it's scary. Okay, so this is like the final question. This is the one that stumped me when Fiona asked me this. And if you don't have an answer now, contact me because I thought about it and I actually wrote her an email afterwards. Okay. Okay, and the question is All American City was used as one designation in the past, what would you call Bloomfield now? What would you use uses a logo for Bloomfield now? See why it stumped me? I think that Bloomfield still an all-American city residentially. As opposed to? But interactively, well, because of the access to the Internet, people are not aware of their actual surroundings. The amount of time that's spent on the various TikTok and Twitter and Facebook and all of that, which I keep reminding my grandchildren, they're not your friends if you've never looked them in the face. That's not what a friend is. Good advice, Grandma. And they get sick of me, but-- Eventually you will be smarter. You know that, don't you? They'll see you as a little smarter than they do right now. Well, I'll tell you what my son and my daughter say when they get together. Isn't it amazing how smart Mom got after we got older? You know the whole Mark Twain saying? Same thing, you got it. I quote that all the time. Anything else you want to talk about? That's what I would-- So do you think if we had more like Sunset Sound series or concerts or interaction that people if people came that that would change the way things are in town? Yeah, I do. I think that we need more interactive stuff. I mean, it's so great to have virtual stuff, but just the idea of actually having interactions. That we're doing. Yes. Which is very special, I think. I think it is. I think this is a very special project. I'm so glad you're a part of it. Well, it attracted me when I first saw it. But I've been very interested in oral histories for a long time, going back to my own family. Have you had oral histories done of certain people in the family? Not as much as-- Just listening to stories? Just listening to stories and handing things down in that way. And we have stories that it makes me feel good to hear my kids quoting them. There's a story about when I was maybe two years old, for Christmas, we always had big Christmas gatherings at our house, and on this particular, I think my parents had bought some new furniture and they got an electric coffee maker, and it was sitting on the end of the buffet in the dining room and it exploded. And as my mother jumped up to clean up the mess my dad said sit down Rachel, that's just the devil trying to ruin our good time. Oh, that is wonderful. We'll clean it up later. That is great. So I hear my kids on occasion saying, don't worry about it, it's just the devil trying to ruin our good time. [Laughs] Then you've done a good job then, you've passed it along. Well we talk about and I'm sure in your family you have the things that you talk about. My grandmother, I grew up with my mother's mother living with us, which was a blessing. Oh, yeah. And my kids, my daughter, my mother died when my daughter was going on seven and her memories of her to this day, because she was so sad after she died, of course, because she was grandma's girl. And she said to me at the time, and she said she missed her so much and all of that. And I said, well, what do you miss the most? She said 'she will make me baked apples.' And I said, 'well, I'll make you a baked apple.' And she said, 'yes, Mommy, but you make them in the microwave.' [Laughter] On that note, anything else you want to say? I think you're doing a great job. Your interviewing style is very interactive. I'm trying very hard to let you do the talking, but it's a-- No, well, you say things that make me think of things, too. That's what we're trying to do. Yeah. And I have that. I have to remember that. I'll turn it off now if you want. Okay.

story.title: Barbara Williams Oral History
story.description: This interview primarily explores the personal history of the interviewee, who is originally from Alabama. Key topics include family background, significant life events, and experiences related to migration during the Jim Crow era. The discussion touches upon the interviewee's formative experiences within a segregated education system, their familial relationships, and their transition to life in the North. There are also reflections on the dynamics of integration and diversity within Bloomfield, as well as the perceived shortcomings of the local education system over the years. The conversation is enriched by personal anecdotes, including the interviewee's family stories and community interactions, providing a nuanced view of both past and present challenges and aspirations in their community.
